question: Explain the order in which catch blocks for base and derived class exceptions should be written and why this order is critically important.
expectedPoints: Catch blocks must be ordered from the most derived (most specific) to the least derived (most general/base class).
Placing a base class catch block before a derived class catch block will make the derived class catch block unreachable.
This is because the exception handling mechanism uses the first compatible handler it finds, and a base class handler is always compatible with a derived class exception.
This ordering ensures that the most specific handler for an exception is used, allowing for precise error handling.

question: Describe a key advantage of using a standard exception hierarchy (e.g., deriving from std::exception) for user-defined exceptions in C++.
expectedPoints: It allows all exceptions, both standard library and user-defined, to be caught by a single catch handler for the base class (e.g., catch (const std::exception& e)).
This promotes a consistent and uniform error handling interface across an entire application.
It enables the use of the standard what() virtual member function to get a descriptive string from any exception in the hierarchy, ensuring polymorphic behavior.
The output of the program will be "Base Exception".

This is due to object slicing: the catch block catches the DerivedException object by value as a BaseException. This copies only the BaseException part of the object, losing the DerivedException-specific information and vtable pointer.
The what() function called is therefore BaseException::what(), not the overridden DerivedException::what().

The correct practice is to catch exceptions by reference (e.g., catch (const BaseException& e)). This prevents slicing and preserves polymorphism, allowing the correct overridden what() function to be called, which would output "Derived Exception".
